author: @SomnoNaut
text: It always bothers me that we send our plastic bottles out for recycling, but yet we buy new plastic to melt in our 3d printers. Why can't household 3d printers use regular recyclable plastic, so we save on ferrying the used plastic around?

author: @Livefreeman
text: not a good idea to 3d print plastic with conductive material because plastic is not conductive, so adding a conductive material will reduce the conductivity of whatever material you are adding, secondly plastic will melt or loose integrity when electric current passess through it. A better option for 3d printing conductive material is to use a conductive pen which you could attach to your 3d printer.

author: @jeffbeck6501
text: This guy has the worst video production values over and over.  Lots of reverb in the room, shitty video, only video of him talking, never showing you close ups, no lighting, no editing, no production at all. I can't even undestand him without putting on closed caption.  He also does everything in a conversation mode as it relates to his life experience.  He does not simply explain some points in a logical way.  It is a long rambling serial communicaiton where you must sift through a lot of talk talk talk to get to few points, and when he does get to the point, he leaves out a ton of detail. He needs to watch his own videos and see how shitty they are compared to other channels. He wants to hold your attention instead of get to the point.

replies.author: @willhatch7721
replies.text: Jeff Beck is that your way of volunteering? I could be wrong but my take here is that we've got a guy who is doing his best to get public interest and funding as he works though his R&D cycle. The videos seem well enough for that, but I agree and have thought of sending Rob some software that will tune out the background noise to improve them.

author: @makersbox5625
text:
sadly you aren't going to get anything usable from this, a 3D printer is rather delicate and it uses brass nozzles, any composite materials in it {graphite/carbon} will clog the printhead. perhaps with a hardened steel nozzle you may get some usability, though even then the filament width needs to be extremely accurate, i fear that rolling it between glass may not achieve that. Of course building an extruder is an option though that can cost up to a 1000 pounds, and even then not give you accurate diameter filament. if you don't believe me, check out this video by Make: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OEkksADFjP8 
Though this stuff would be great for injection moulding, check out https://preciousplastic.com/en/ for some info on it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @DanielbenDaniel
text: Now you are talking my language Robert.. :D I posted videos on my page, or what ever you call it..?  I mixed graphite powder with a high carbon black colorant additive. HDPE, LLDPE & PP(Poly Propylene) blends.  I'v compounded blends in my 70mm screw in my blown film extruder. The high pressure (10,000psi) temperatures over 400f and CTM mixing zone on my extruder is helping to bust up the graphite making graphine layers. The problem is high loads of graphite work as lubricant in my extruder. I have yet to achieve a high enough load for highly conductivity films, But I am working with some new carrier materials that are looking rather promising.

author: @MongrelShark
text: After melting in solvent, and then going through a filament extruder. I suspect its going to be brittle be a little low on plasticizer. What could I add to the solvent or filament extruder as a plasticizer? I'm thinking the detergent I added to concrete as plasticizer wouldn't come out so good with plastic?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i will make an extruder and try this but it came out well for me - probably because poly morph isn't polycaprylacton alone but already contains plasticisers as it is a product meant for melting and remelting - but if you are worried try glycerine that's the standard plastcs plasticiser

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text:
Been hearing stories of people trying to remelt  failed prints into new filament and it not printing well second time around. Or 4th melt if you think about it.
I'd also like to be able to re use plastic rubbish So plasticiser would likely be needed there too. Thanks for the tip.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Mongrel Shark
no worries mate
